title: Gestational Age Calculator (GA in Weeks + Days) 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
description: 🩺 Clinical gestational age calculator. Enter LMP or CRL from a first-trimester ultrasound and get exact GA in weeks+days, trimester, and ACOG-compliant EDD. Free.
intro: Compute the **gestational age (GA)** of a pregnancy in the clinical format **weeks + days** (e.g. *15 2/7*). Input your **last menstrual period (LMP)** and the calculator returns your exact **GA today**, current **trimester** (ACOG definition), and the corresponding **estimated due date (EDD)**. Intended for both expectant parents and health-care workers who need precise dating.
keyTakeaway: **Gestational age** is counted from **day 0 = first day of LMP**, not from conception. Clinical format is **weeks + days** (range 0 0/7 to ~41 6/7). Each day matters: many screening tests have narrow windows (e.g. nuchal translucency is **11 0/7 – 13 6/7**).
useCases: A healthcare worker needs to record the GA in the mother's chart to schedule tests.
You want to know if you're within the window for a specific screening (NT scan, quad screen, anatomy scan).
You had an early ultrasound and want to cross-check CRL-based dating against LMP-based.
You're tracking embryo development week by week using obstetric reference charts.
You need to convert "months pregnant" to "weeks + days" for official forms.

fields.label: First day of last menstrual period (LMP)
fields.help: Gestational age is counted from LMP day 1. Enter the calendar date of the first day of your last period.

dataUpdate.source: ACOG Committee Opinion 700 — Methods for Estimating the Due Date
dataUpdate.notes: Based on ACOG's standardized redating thresholds for first/second/third trimester ultrasounds.

example.title: Example: LMP January 15, 2026, today April 22
steps: **GA in weeks + days**: 97 / 7 = **13 weeks + 6 days** (written as *13 6/7*).
example.result: **GA today: 13 6/7** — last day of the 1st trimester. EDD **October 22, 2026**. Window for NT scan (11 0/7 – 13 6/7) closes tonight at midnight.

explanation:
## What exactly is gestational age?

**Gestational age (GA)** is the time elapsed since the **first day of the last menstrual period (LMP)**, measured in **weeks + days** (0 0/7 … 42+ 0/7). It's distinct from **embryonic age** (which counts from conception/ovulation, ≈2 weeks later) and from **fetal age** (which typically equals embryonic age + 2 weeks).

Convention: when a chart says *15 3/7*, the pregnancy is 15 complete weeks + 3 additional days. `/7` is always the denominator.

## How clinicians date a pregnancy

**ACOG (2017)** ranks the dating methods by accuracy:

1. **First-trimester ultrasound (≤13 6/7) with CRL measurement** — most accurate (±5 to 7 days).
2. **LMP when the LMP date is certain and cycles are regular (28 days)**.
3. **Second-trimester ultrasound (14 0/7 – 21 6/7)** — ±7 to 10 days.
4. **Third-trimester ultrasound** — ±2 to 3 weeks (least reliable).

This calculator uses **method 2 (LMP-based dating)**. If your early ultrasound dates the pregnancy >7 days differently than LMP (in T1), obstetricians **redate to the ultrasound**. See ACOG's redating table below.

## ACOG redating thresholds

| Ultrasound window | Redate if discrepancy > |
|---|---|
| ≤ 8 6/7 | 5 days |
| 9 0/7 – 13 6/7 | 7 days |
| 14 0/7 – 15 6/7 | 7 days |
| 16 0/7 – 21 6/7 | 10 days |
| 22 0/7 – 27 6/7 | 14 days |
| ≥ 28 0/7 | 21 days |

## Screening windows that depend on exact GA

- **Nuchal translucency (NT)**: 11 0/7 – 13 6/7.
- **cfDNA / NIPT**: ≥ 10 0/7 (earlier may have high no-call rate).
- **Quad screen**: 15 0/7 – 22 6/7 (ideal 16–18).
- **Anatomy scan (Level II)**: 18 0/7 – 22 0/7 (ideal 20).
- **GBS culture**: 36 0/7 – 37 6/7.
- **Induction for post-term**: typically offered at 41 0/7.

Missing a window by a day can mean the test must be rescheduled or replaced. That's why GA is recorded in **weeks + days**, not just weeks.

## Converting GA to months (for questions like "how many months pregnant?")

40 weeks / 9 months is approximate. Using **lunar months of 4 weeks**:

| GA | Months |
|---|---|
| 0 0/7 – 4 6/7 | 1 month |
| 5 0/7 – 8 6/7 | 2 months |
| 9 0/7 – 13 6/7 | 3 months (end of T1) |
| 14 0/7 – 17 6/7 | 4 months |
| 18 0/7 – 22 6/7 | 5 months |
| 23 0/7 – 27 6/7 | 6 months (end of T2) |
| 28 0/7 – 31 6/7 | 7 months |
| 32 0/7 – 35 6/7 | 8 months |
| 36 0/7 – 40 0/7 | 9 months |

## When LMP-based dating fails

- **Irregular cycles** (PCOS, perimenopause, postpartum breastfeeding).
- **Recent hormonal contraception** (ovulation resumes unpredictably).
- **Cycle length ≠ 28 days**: if you ovulate late (day 18 in a 32-day cycle), LMP+280 underestimates EDD by ≈4 days.
- **Unknown LMP** (bleeding on implantation mistaken for a period, or no recorded LMP).

In any of these, ask for a **first-trimester dating scan**. It's the single most accurate tool in obstetrics.

## Sources

- ACOG Committee Opinion 700 (2017) — *Methods for Estimating the Due Date*.
- Robinson HP, Fleming JE (1975). *A critical evaluation of sonar crown-rump length measurements*. Br J Obstet Gynaecol.
- WHO — Global standards for gestational age.

faq.q: What does gestational age of 28 3/7 mean?
faq.a: **28 weeks + 3 days** since the first day of the last menstrual period. The first number is complete weeks, the slash-7 is additional days (0–6). At 28 3/7 you are just entering the third trimester.

faq.q: How is gestational age different from fetal age?
faq.a: **Gestational age** counts from the first day of LMP (conception happens around week 2). **Fetal age** (or **embryonic age**) counts from conception, so it's typically **2 weeks less** than GA. GA is the standard in medicine; fetal age is more often used in embryology texts.

faq.q: How does a CRL ultrasound calculate gestational age?
faq.a: The **crown-rump length (CRL)** is measured in millimeters on a first-trimester ultrasound. Using the Robinson-Fleming formula, the scanner computes GA as **GA (days) = 8.052 × √CRL + 23.73**. This method is accurate to ±5 days in the first trimester and is preferred over LMP when they disagree by more than 7 days (ACOG).
They're the same thing. Both count from the first day of the last menstrual period. Menstrual age is the older term; gestational age is the modern standard.

faq.q: Why do doctors count from LMP instead of from conception?
faq.a: Because the exact day of conception is usually unknown, while LMP is a self-reported date that most women remember. Adding 14 days (typical ovulation in a 28-day cycle) to LMP would also work, but LMP is used directly for consistency across all pregnancies.

faq.q: What if my cycle is 35 days, not 28?
faq.a: LMP-based GA will be **inaccurate by a few days** because ovulation in a 35-day cycle is around day 21, not day 14. Request a first-trimester ultrasound for accurate dating. The OB may adjust your EDD by 5–7 days.

faq.q: Can this calculator be used after an IVF cycle?
faq.a: For IVF, dating is even simpler: the **transfer date is known exactly**. If you had a 5-day embryo (blastocyst) transfer, your LMP equivalent is **transfer date − 19 days** (14 for ovulation + 5 for blastocyst age). Enter that synthetic LMP to get the correct GA.
ACOG defines **full-term** as **39 0/7 – 40 6/7 weeks**. **Early term** is 37 0/7 – 38 6/7, **late term** is 41 0/7 – 41 6/7, and **post-term** is 42+. Preterm is anything before 37 0/7.

sources.name: ACOG Committee Opinion 700 — Methods for Estimating the Due Date
WHO — Maternal health and gestational age standards

howToSteps.name: Enter your LMP
howToSteps.text: Select the first day of your last menstrual period — this is day 0 of GA week 1.

howToSteps.name: Read your GA in weeks + days
howToSteps.text: The primary result uses the clinical format, e.g. *15 2/7* = 15 weeks and 2 days.

howToSteps.name: Cross-check with ultrasound if needed
howToSteps.text: If you have an early-pregnancy ultrasound with a CRL measurement, compare EDD values. ACOG redates when the discrepancy exceeds 7 days in T1.

howToSteps.name: Use the trimester and EDD for planning
howToSteps.text: The calculator shows when the 2nd and 3rd trimesters start so you can schedule screenings and appointments.
